name: consistent
statement_text:
A general linear method (A, U, B, V ) is ‘consistent’ if it is
preconsistent with preconsistency vector u and there exists a vector v such that
                                   B1 + V v = u + v.                          (510c)

  Just as for linear multistep methods, we need a concept of stability. In the
general linear case this is defined in terms of the power-boundedness of V and,
as we shall see, is related to the solvability of the problem y  = 0.

variables.name: A
variables.definition: matrix in the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: U
variables.definition: matrix in the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: B
variables.definition: matrix in the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: V
variables.definition: matrix in the general linear method (A, U, B, V)

variables.name: u
variables.definition: preconsistency vector, an eigenvector of V with eigenvalue 1 satisfying U u = 1

variables.name: v
variables.definition: vector related to the method's accuracy, satisfying B1 + V v = u + v

preamble: The text discusses general linear methods defined by matrices (A, U, B, V) for solving ODEs, focusing on conditions for preconsistency and consistency. Preconsistency requires a vector u such that V u = u and U u = 1, ensuring the method can solve y' = 1 exactly. Consistency adds a vector v satisfying B1 + V v = u + v, related to second-order accuracy.

dependencies.name: stability in the sense of Dahlquist
